content: The site of Indus Valley civilization contains various types of pottery, including Harappan pottery and variant Harappan pottery. The sites also have burials, such as those found on the lower slopes of the tell at Nal. Additionally, the sites have occupation layers, with discussions ongoing about which came first, the burials or the occupation. Other items found include ceramics with a range of polychromy, such as black, brown, red, yellow, blue, and green.

    Women are represented in art primarily in connection with fertility and fecundity. Women, whether with or without children, are depicted lying on beds, which relates to the idea of fecundity, and other figures are shown seated. Exceptionally, an emphatic representation of a female vulva was found from Periano Ghundai in northern Baluchistan, but this is not typical of the Indus cultures.

Based on the artistic figures and representations found, women are depicted primarily in connection with the themes of fertility and fecundity. Occasionally, lumps of clay are added to figures to represent infants at the breast or on the hip, and pregnant figures indicate a general notion of fertility, either in thanksgiving or anticipation. Unlike the art associated with Mother Goddess cults, these representations generally do not emphasize generative organs. Women, with or without children, are depicted lying on beds, a posture related to the idea of fecundity, while other figures are shown seated. An emphatic representation of a female vulva was found at Periano Ghundai in northern Baluchistan, but this is considered very exceptional for the chalcolithic cultures of the Indus and its borders.
